title: Timepoint 1 — Admission, variceal bleed/TIPS, and new post-procedure fevers
scenarioBrief: Janet Brooks is a 55-year-old woman with alcohol-related cirrhosis who presents with 3 months of ~20 lb involuntary weight loss, new ascites (paracentesis negative for SBP), and bilateral pleural effusions. Shortly after admission she has massive hematemesis; she is intubated, found to have bleeding varices (banding failed), and undergoes emergent TIPS with control of bleeding (ceftriaxone started for prophylaxis). Now, post-TIPS, she has developed high fevers and hypotension requiring a vasopressor and remains somnolent off sedation. Blood cultures are drawn and antibiotics are broadened; the workup is just beginning.

chartGate._notes: TP1 visibility: the outpatient weight-loss workup notes/labs, the ED H&P, the ICU admission + EGD/TIPS note, and the early post-TIPS progress note (fevers begin, workup initiated). The later negative results, the ID consult, the CMO deliberation, and the recovery/drug-fever reveal are NOT yet visible.

prompts.question: How would you evaluate this patient's involuntary weight loss?

essential: Thorough history (dietary intake, GI symptoms, alcohol, mood/depression, social/functional barriers to eating) and a directed physical exam
In cirrhosis specifically, evaluate for HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA — dedicated multiphase liver imaging (CT or MRI) and AFP
Pursue a broader malignancy / age-appropriate cancer screen (given smoking and family history) plus basic labs (CBC, CMP, TSH)
Assess nutrition/malnutrition (a major contributor in cirrhosis) and reversible contributors (alcohol, depression, medications)
bonus: Notes the mildly elevated AFP already on file → prioritize HCC-surveillance imaging
Organizes a structured differential (malignancy, malabsorption, chronic-disease cachexia, psychiatric/social, endocrine)
Reviews and builds on the outpatient workup already started rather than duplicating it
redFlags: Anchoring on a single explanation; omitting malignancy (especially HCC) in a cirrhotic with weight loss
No imaging/AFP plan for HCC despite cirrhosis

prompts.question: What is your approach to evaluating her fevers?

essential: A systematic infectious workup appropriate to a post-procedure cirrhotic: blood cultures, DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS to rule out SBP, urinalysis/culture, chest imaging, line/site evaluation, and consideration of procedure-related sources
Reasonable empiric broad-spectrum coverage WHILE culturing — explicitly coupled with a plan to RE-EVALUATE and de-escalate based on results
Keeps a BROAD differential that includes NON-INFECTIOUS causes of fever from the outset (drug fever, transfusion reaction, VTE, the recent procedure, alcoholic hepatitis) — not infection only
States that an unrevealing workup should prompt reconsidering the diagnosis rather than reflexively broadening antibiotics
bonus: Explicitly enumerates non-infectious fever causes alongside infection
Separates the altered mental status (e.g., hepatic encephalopathy, TIPS-related) from the fever workup
Sets de-escalation / stopping criteria up front
Considers the timeline (fevers began around the antibiotic broadening) as data
redFlags: An infection-only approach with no plan to reconsider if the workup is negative
Reflexively escalating antibiotics without a structured differential
Omitting a diagnostic paracentesis in a febrile cirrhotic with ascites
